exercises.title: start() vs run() — which creates a thread?
exercises.scenario: run() is just a method call; start() creates a new thread.
hints: run() executes on the calling thread — still 'main'
start() creates a new thread — named 'Thread-0' by default

exercises.title: Create thread with lambda
exercises.scenario: Lambda as Runnable — most concise thread creation.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank to create and start a thread using a lambda.

blanks.hint: Method that creates a new thread

hints: No-param lambda: () ->
Always call start(), never run()

exercises.title: Three ways to create a thread
exercises.scenario: All three creation methods produce equivalent threads.
exercises.instructions:
Create and start one thread each way: (1) extend Thread, (2) implement Runnable, (3) lambda. Each should print its thread name.

Expected output contains three lines like:
Thread via extend: Thread-N
Thread via Runnable: Thread-N
Thread via lambda: Thread-N
hints: All three use start()
join() waits for completion so output appears before main exits

exercises.title: Thread naming for better logs
exercises.scenario: Named threads produce meaningful log output.
exercises.instructions:
Create 3 threads named 'Worker-1', 'Worker-2', 'Worker-3'. Each prints its name. Join all.

Expected output contains:
Worker-1 running
Worker-2 running
Worker-3 running
(order may vary)
hints: t.setName('Worker-N') before t.start()
Start all first, then join all

exercises.title: Thread.sleep and timing
exercises.scenario: Sleep pauses the current thread — demonstrates timing.
exercises.instructions:
Create two threads: Thread-A sleeps 300ms and prints 'A done', Thread-B sleeps 100ms and prints 'B done'. Start both, join both. B should always finish first.

Expected output:
B done
A done
Both done
exercises.starter_code: // Two threads with different sleep durations

test_cases.expected_stdout:
B done
A done
Both done

hints: B sleeps 100ms, A sleeps 300ms — B finishes first
Start both BEFORE joining either
Always handle InterruptedException

exercises.title: join() for ordered completion
exercises.scenario: join() makes calling thread wait for target thread to finish.
exercises.instructions:
Start a background thread that takes 200ms. Use join() to guarantee main waits. Print 'Background started', 'Background done', 'Main continues' in that exact order.

Expected output:
Background started
Background done
Main continues

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Background started
Background done
Main continues

hints: bg.join() blocks main until bg's run() returns
'Main continues' prints AFTER join() returns — guaranteed

exercises.title: Parallel tasks — measure speedup
exercises.scenario: Three tasks in parallel vs sequential — measure time difference.
exercises.instructions:
Three tasks each sleeping 500ms. Time them:
1. Sequential (in main thread, 3 × sleep)
2. Parallel (3 threads started simultaneously)

Print both times. Parallel should be ~500ms, sequential ~1500ms.

Expected output (approx):
Sequential: ~1500ms
Parallel: ~500ms
hints: Sequential: three sleep(500) in main thread = 1500ms
Parallel: start all three first, then join all = ~500ms
Method reference App::doWork as Runnable

exercises.title: Thread lifecycle states
exercises.scenario: Observe thread states through lifecycle transitions.
exercises.instructions:
Create a thread that sleeps 300ms. Print its state: before start, after start (immediately), after join.

Expected output:
Before start: NEW
After start: RUNNABLE or TIMED_WAITING
After join: TERMINATED
hints: NEW before start()
After start: RUNNABLE if not yet sleeping, TIMED_WAITING if already asleep
TERMINATED after join()

exercises.title: Non-determinism demo
exercises.scenario: Thread execution order is unpredictable — show it.
exercises.instructions:
Start 5 threads, each printing their number. Run 3 times (in a loop). Show that order varies. Note: print 'Run N:' before each set.

Expected output (order within each run varies):
Run 1:
[some order of 0-4]
Run 2:
[possibly different order]
Run 3:
[possibly different order]
exercises.starter_code: // Non-determinism: thread order varies per run
hints: Start all in first loop, join all in second loop
The integer order within each run is non-deterministic
effectively final: final int n = i

exercises.title: Daemon thread
exercises.scenario: Daemon threads stop when main thread exits.
exercises.instructions:
Create a daemon thread that prints a message every 200ms indefinitely. Let main sleep 500ms then exit. Show the daemon stops automatically when main finishes.

Expected output (approx 2-3 daemon messages, then main exits):
Daemon tick
Daemon tick
Main done — JVM will exit
[daemon stops]
exercises.starter_code: // Daemon thread stops when JVM exits
hints: setDaemon(true) BEFORE start()
JVM exits when only daemon threads remain
No join() — main just exits and daemon dies with it

exercises.title: Parallel file simulator — concurrent processing
exercises.instructions:
Simulate processing 5 files. Each takes a random time (100-500ms). Process all concurrently. Print when each finishes. Join all and print total time.

Expected output (order varies, time ~500ms not 2500ms):
File-1 done (Xms)
File-3 done (Yms)
...
All files processed in ~Zms
hints: Create all threads in first loop, start all in second, join all in third
Named via Thread constructor: new Thread(runnable, 'name')
Total time should be ~500ms (the longest delay)

exercises.title: join() timeout behavior
exercises.scenario: join(ms) returns after timeout even if thread is still running.
exercises.expected_solution:
State after join(200): TIMED_WAITING
Thread finished
State after join(): TERMINATED

test_cases.expected:
State after join(200): TIMED_WAITING
Thread finished
State after join(): TERMINATED

hints: join(200) returns after 200ms — thread is still sleeping (1000ms)
Thread still in TIMED_WAITING (sleeping for 800 more ms)
join() waits for completion → then TERMINATED

exercises.title: Thread pool simulation
exercises.scenario: Manage a fixed number of worker threads for multiple tasks.
exercises.instructions:
Simulate processing 8 tasks with only 3 worker threads. Each task prints 'Worker-N processing task-M'. Workers pick up new tasks when available.

Use a simple array of 3 threads, each processing 2-3 tasks sequentially.

Expected output (order varies within batches):
[3 threads start, processing 8 tasks total, 8 completion messages]
hints: LinkedBlockingQueue.poll() returns null when queue is empty
Each worker loops: poll task → process → repeat until null
This is a preview of Topic 10.3 (ExecutorService does this automatically)

exercises.title: Restaurant kitchen — full simulation
exercises.scenario: The worked example extended with error handling and reporting.
exercises.instructions:
Build a kitchen simulation with:
- 4 stations: Drinks(200ms), Starter(400ms), Main(800ms), Dessert(300ms)
- All start simultaneously
- Main thread waits for all with join()
- Print when each station finishes, in order of completion
- Print total time

Expected output (in completion order):
Drinks ready (200ms)
Dessert ready (300ms)
Starter ready (400ms)
Main ready (800ms)
All served in ~800ms
hints: Each station records its own start time inside run()
Start all 4, then join all 4
Output order: completion order (fastest first)

exercises.title: Parallel search — find first match
exercises.scenario: Search a large range in parallel across multiple threads.
exercises.instructions:
Search for a value in a range 1-1000 using 4 threads, each searching a quarter. First thread to find the target reports it. Use a shared flag (`volatile boolean found`) to stop other threads early.

Search for target = 573. Expected:

Thread-N found 573 in range [X, Y]
Search complete
hints: volatile boolean found — shared flag (volatile explained in Topic 10.2)
Each thread checks !found before each iteration — stops early
573 is in range [501, 750] — Thread-3 will find it
